Transcrição Completa: 3 Youtube Ads with Brian Moncada
[00:00]
Thank you. [applause] Thank you. Thank you, Jeremy. What's up, guys? How you guys doing?
[00:08]
Good. Um, quick survey. Who's running YouTube ads right now for any of their clients? One, two, three. No one else for their business. Oh, four. Nice. Sick. Okay, cool.
[00:20]
Um, awesome. Yeah, Jeremy, too, obviously. Yeah. Um, beautiful. So, um, it's funny. I just spent the last three months, uh, living abroad in Colombia. Medellín, who's been? Anybody else been? Beautiful country, right?
[00:38]
And um has anybody here got LASIK surgery on their eyes? Okay, two people. Um who wants to get LASIK? Anybody else want to get LASIK that wears glasses? Yeah. Okay.
[00:52]
So, the reason why I say that and I asked that is because while I was over there um I was going to get LASIK. So, I've been wearing glasses since I was in high school. I was addicted to a game called Runescape, actually.
[01:08]
And uh I was maxed out on that game. Had like 144 level. I was like 134, I think, like 99 attack, 99 strength, 99 defense, 99 range, like just total nerd in high school, right?
[01:24]
But, [snorts] uh, my eyes are fucked because of that, right? Because I just spent all day just playing that game. And so, when I was in Colombia, um, I was going to get LASIK.
[01:38]
Now, I was going through the tests to get LASIK to check my eyes to make sure that I was going to be good. My wife got it a year before from the same doctor I was going to get it at in Medellín, by the way.
[01:52]
Now, when I was getting the tests, the doctor checked one of the last tests I was supposed to be doing. And he looked at the monitor after the last test and he's like, "Okay, everything looks good. You know, you got a good cornea, good size, you should be able to cut it. No problem. You should be good."
[02:12]
But there's this thing that I'm seeing, this little speck in the corner of the machine that's just making me want to do the test again. So, let's just run the test again.
[02:22]
We did the test again, and the thing, the little speck, this little yellow speck in the monitor shows up again. and he goes, "You know, the machine's telling me you should probably do PRK." Does anyone know what PRK is?
[02:38]
Okay, so how was that?
[02:40]
Uh, so with LASIK, what they do is they like cut it flat, do the laser, close it up. PRK, they take what looks like an electric toothbrush that's made entirely in metal, and they scrape your cornea to flatten it.
[02:56]
And then they use an impulse, like a high impulse laser to reshape it. So instead of like going to bed and having perfect vision the next day, you're on drugs and blind for five days.
[03:08]
So that doesn't sound fun, does it? That sounds very...
[03:12]
Well. Okay, there you go. Right. But again, keep in mind, I'm abroad, right? Still practicing my Spanish and uh you know, trying to make sure I'm making a right decision here.
[03:24]
My wife's with me, you know, she's translating whatever, but I don't feel good about the decision. I'm not confident in the decision. And this guy, this doctor who's been doing it for years, says, "I've seen thousands of eyes. The machine tells me sometimes things that I don't agree with. We can still do LASIK though."
[03:44]
And I'm like, you know what? Let me sleep on it. Right. I go home that night and I'm looking on YouTube. PRK versus LASIK. LASIK versus PRK. Uh LASIK versus whatever. Like, is PRK worth it? Is LASIK safe?
[04:00]
I'm typing in all these search queries on YouTube, and I get hit with a fucking YouTube ad where the hook says, "If you're about to get LASIK surgery, don't do it before you watch this video." That's the hook in the first five seconds.
[04:16]
Obviously, I watched the whole video. It's a VSL turning people off of LASIK to instead pursue alternative natural methods to heal their eyes.
[04:26]
And needless to say, I didn't get LASIK that Friday and I got a refund that I didn't end up going through the surgery. And it was because not really because of the YouTube ad. That didn't help me at that time.
[04:40]
But that story is powerful because all of the customers that you guys have that you run ads for your clients are searching for certain things. They're raising their hands saying, "I have problems." Right?
[04:54]
I was going to get LASIK surgery. I had questions on the surgery, right? And at the moment I needed an answer, I got hit with a YouTube ad that said, "If you're about to get LASIK surgery, don't before you watch this video." That's powerful, right?
[05:10]
It ultimately cost that doctor $500 in Colombia, which is very cheap to get LASIK. $500 total, by the way. Uh, and so naturally it cost me his business. It cost that ad cost him my business.
[05:26]
And so now I'm going to get a second opinion. I'm not wearing any contacts or glasses right now. So I'm trying to just fix my eyesight just by doing it naturally and just trying to stare at shit. So we'll see how that goes. I'll let you guys know.
[05:40]
Worked.
[05:42]
Uh, the YouTube ad worked. And that's the whole premise of this story and this presentation, which sets it up pretty good, I think. So hopefully that gives you the power of where YouTube is and what we're going to be covering.

[06:18]

[07:26]
I worked for him for almost three years. I learned direct response marketing from him, learned sales funnels, copywriting, was mentored by him for almost a year and a half personally and then we grew the company to where the time I got with him was more limited.
[07:42]
But ultimately, I helped him scale his brands and businesses with YouTube ads. And I earned I did I didn't earn but I was a part of earning a two comma club award for his business at that time.
[07:56]
Uh at my prime I was spending $50,000 a day on one offer for him uh across we had multiple offers but the one offer that we were scaling the most was his millionaire success habits book. Does anyone remember that book?
[08:14]
That was the one that I was running YouTube ads for. That's the one that got up to $50,000 a day at its prime. And that continued around for about three months at its peak.
[08:26]
So I would wake up every single day. I would refresh my Google Ads account on my phone and I would just see 40 grand spent yesterday. CPA's $25 average order value was $40. Good day.
[08:42]
Some days we were $5 in the negative. And obviously if you're spending 50 grand, it's a lot that you can eat uh in terms of cost that would keep me up at night and paranoid.
[08:54]
So I learned pretty quickly how to deal with my emotions spending that kind of money on ads. And um that's where I got my start.

[12:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Keyword Targeting REMOVED"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

So what this means is you used to be able to type in a keyword. For example, with Dean, one of the best keywords we used was the law of attraction. Okay?
[12:50]

[13:04]
However, YouTube just changed this to where now you can no longer target keywords with your YouTube ads that are video conversion campaigns.
[13:14]
So, has is anyone here still running or trying to run keyword campaigns or was running keyword campaigns?
[13:22]
Was right and they started declining probably really recently. If you go into them, if you have a keyword campaign that's still running, you should take a look at it because it's definitely open targeting and you're targeting anyone and everyone.
[13:36]
You're not targeting that keyword anymore because they just removed all the keywords.
[13:42]
Brian, something that you just said, um, I actually want to bring it back to that cuz you said it in passing, but it's such a key point.
[13:50]

[13:54]
Yep.
[13:55]
Keywords because law of attraction people want to make more money.
[14:00]

[14:10]
Interesting.
[14:11]

[14:14]

[14:15]

[14:20]

[14:22]

[14:26]

[14:32]

[14:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Similar Audiences REMOVED August 1"
NÚMEROS: August 1 (data de remoção)
VISUAL: Calendário destacando 1º de agosto, ícone de alerta, comparação entre Similar Audiences e Optimized Targeting
[14:50]
And so, the next update is similar audiences will be removed August 1. So, Facebook lookalikes, those are obviously really good audiences you can target. With Google and with YouTube, it's similar audiences.
[15:06]
Those would be amazing. You know, I would uh always love to see our similar audiences be created in our Google Ads account for our clients because if we uploaded a customer list, automatically Google would create a similar audience of 10,000 to 100,000 people that are similar to the people that have already bought the book or already bought our clients' shit right?
[15:28]
Um those are no longer a thing. Now, August 1st, it will be optimized targeting, which is just a click of a button to say, "Google, help me find more people that are going to be optimized towards this specific conversion action if it's a lead, if it's a call."
[15:44]
So, keep aware, keep an eye on that. It's obviously coming up next month, but you can no longer target similar audiences as of August first.

[16:26]
So, if someone's watching your video on mobile device, Google will automatically convert it to be a better viewable experience for them, right? So, you can check that on or off. We're split testing this right now.
[16:38]
Uh, with it on in a few accounts, it's doing well. With it off in a few accounts, it's doing well. So, like it's brand new and there's really no data to prove this. This just happened like literally last week.

[18:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Google Ads Ecosystem"
NÚMEROS: 3 formatos principais
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando YouTube Ads no topo (top of funnel), Google Discovery no meio, Search Ads e Shopping na base, ícones representando cada plataforma, setas indicando fluxo de funil
[18:32]
Okay, you have YouTube ads, which is top of funnel, right? This is the best way to get more awareness and brand recognition for your clients. Uh there's three different types of formats you can see there, right?
[18:46]
So the one in the middle on the very top is the in-stream, the one that you're used to skipping. The one to the right is shown on a third-party website that someone sees on Yahoo and they click on it, it goes to a video.
[19:00]
The one on the left is the skippable one, but the middle one, I'm sorry, is the one that you can see in your feed as if it's a regular YouTube video, but it's actually an ad.
[19:12]
But that's just one platform in the Google Ads ecosystem. Okay. On the left-hand side, you have Google Discovery Ads. This is good for middle of funnel remarketing after they've seen the YouTube ad.
[19:26]
You remarket them on Google Discovery with uh ads that show on their Gmail, right? Ads that show on their YouTube channel, that's a banner. And then ads that show up on third-party websites as well.
[19:40]
We always use those for middle of funnel. We never go cold traffic with those for all of our clients because most of the time they just going to get piss poor results.
[19:50]
And then the bottom here, you have search ads. One of the easiest campaigns anyone here can set up for their agency as well or their clients' businesses are search ads, branded keywords.
[20:04]
Uh for Brandon Carter, we have Brandon Carter as a keyword on his search campaign. So whenever someone searches for Brandon Carter or King Keto or, you know, high ticket trainer or whatever his keywords for his brand are, we'll put those as a search ad and then his funnel pops up to the very top of the search results.
[20:26]
This is also an easy way for you to kick out any competitors who are bidding on your stuff as well. And then we have uh shopping there. So I don't do any ecom really. We always primarily focus on info products, but you have shopping as well.
[20:42]
And the reason why I show this is because again, YouTube ads is just 1/4th of the things you can run. And you've got to be leveraging at least the top two right here in the middle, the straight line, and then the one on the left. That's what we always do.
[20:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Markets for YouTube Ads"
NÚMEROS: TAM (Total Addressable Market) 1M+ minimum
VISUAL: Lista de nichos ideais - Health & Fitness, Dating & Relationships, Make Money Online, Biz Op, ícones representando cada categoria, destaque para mercados de massa B2C
[21:00]
Um, and so now that you have the overview, what I found is that YouTube ads is primarily best for mass market B2C offers. Okay, these are health and fitness offers, dating and relationships, make money online, and biz op.
[21:16]
Okay, it's a scaling platform. If you're trying to target, for example, B2B where the TAM is just small, it's just not going to work.
[21:26]
The B2B offers that can work are agency owners, coaches, consultants, course creators. Those are the typical ones that we all know and love.
[21:36]
But it will not work if you're trying to run YouTube ads for, for example, someone came to me and said, "Hey, I'm trying to target car dealership owners in Germany."
[21:46]
And after doing research, there's only about 22,000 of those people in existence. I'm not going to run YouTube ads for that offer. Uh, and the client couldn't pay me to do that because it's not going to work. There's better ways to get in front of those people.
[22:04]
Uh just like with for example independent tax professionals which we did try and I'm saying this because we learned and there's only 80,000 of those people in existence and they're all like 50 plus primarily. They're not watching YouTube videos really. Go ahead.

[22:28]

[22:32]
A million plus.
[22:33]
Yeah. A million plus. It's not to say, look, again, I'm talking from experience of scaling though, right? You can run ads on YouTube to independent tax professionals. You're not going to be able to spend more than 100 bucks a day.
[22:48]

[24:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Offer Types That Work"
NÚMEROS: $1 (lowest offer), $7.95 (shipping), $27 (low ticket info), $45-$55 AOV, 2x ROI
VISUAL: Escala de ofertas desde low ticket até high ticket, exemplos de preços, ícones de dinheiro, gráfico mostrendo progressão de valor
[24:14]
So, to your question, Mark, um, you know, lowest I would say, I mean, there's people, uh, what's his name? Um, this dude with dreads, he's doing a $1 ecom case study offer right now on YouTube. Oh... He's friends with like um, Sebastian Georgie.
[24:34]
Jordan Welch. Jordan Welch, he's running a $1, yeah, ecom case study offer. So, the lowest could be as simple as $1. The other offer is free. Free plus shipping book offer for seven bucks, right?
[24:50]
So, low ticket can work on YouTube, right? This is exactly what we did for Dean when I was working for him full-time. And the reason why it works so well for Dean is because Dean had the high ticket backend already in place.
[25:06]
He just needed more customers to feed his sales team. So, when we scaled Millionaire Success Habits, it was a free plus shipping book offer, but you paid $7.95 for shipping and then, you know, you have upsells, you have OTOs, you have the thank you page, then you have a sales call on the back end.
[25:26]
So, low ticket can work. It's a big myth. People think YouTube you can only run high ticket. You can run low ticket. The problem though is most info businesses don't have a high ticket backend or a dialed in sales team to be able to make it work.
[25:44]
And then that's already getting them to a million a month on its own. So, like introducing a low ticket doesn't make sense until you're at that point. It's just going to be way harder.
[25:54]
We had a client, the Tapp Brothers, who I'll show you a case study in a second. They were selling a $27 low ticket info product for everyday people that want to build muscle at their home without any weights, without any equipment.
[26:10]
And we were profitable on that by about 2x, right? So, we were spending about $30 to get a customer. And we were getting about $45-$50, sometimes $55 average order value.
[26:24]
But after a year of running that, the profits declined, the ads started rising, the ad costs, and they didn't have any backend. So eventually that offer died because there was no backend. Does that make sense?
[26:38]
One more question on this cuz my wife, she's got an e-commerce business, right? and we've got like um I'm basically I'm trying to figure out she's got a webinar which sells a $297 product and her target market is African-American women with curly hair.
[26:56]
And I'm wondering and we were like wondering like could we run YouTube ads to this webinar funnel uh for the $297 offer and then on the back end we've got other products like hair conditioner shampoo the lifetime value of a customer is about $1,880 if you just buy the shampoos.
[27:16]
So it's you'll probably get close to that on the cost per acquisition. You'll get like $100-$150 cost per acquisition really I would imagine.
[27:26]
Yeah.
[27:27]
But so it might work on the webinar thing the $297.
[27:32]
Yeah it would definitely work. It would probably cost you about $150 cost per acquisition for that offer.
[27:38]
Oh we could do that.
[27:39]
Yeah.
[27:40]
Okay. All right.
[27:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High Ticket Funnel Structure"

[27:44]

[28:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "KPIs and Benchmarks - Front End Ad Metrics"
NÚMEROS: 1% CTR minimum, 2%+ ideal CTR, 20%+ view rate, $3-$5 CPC (US), $1-$2 CPC (outside US)
VISUAL: Tabela com métricas destacadas, números em destaque com cores diferentes para mínimo vs ideal, ícones de performance
[28:02]
Um, and so for some KPIs and benchmarks, I know this is hard to read, but basically I'm talking about the front-end ad metrics here. So, three things you should look out for, okay, with your YouTube ads specifically to know if your ads are the bottleneck or they're working, right?
[28:20]
So, the click-through rate, very easy here, but you should aim to get at least 1% click-through rate on your YouTube ads. Ideally, 2% or higher. And I'll show you a case study of why this makes so much sense.
[28:34]
And then view rate 20% or higher. Okay, view rate 20% or higher. And then cost per click, you're going to average $3 to $5 in the US market. Cost per click is $3 to $5 in the US market and then $1 to $2 outside the US.
[28:52]
So those are the benchmarks you should aim for.
[28:56]
If your lead and like call booking costs look good, but all those numbers look bad. Do you care about those numbers at all?
[29:04]
No.
[29:05]
Yeah. If everything's profitable, like you work backwards, right? If you're 4x, you spend more.
[29:10]
Those are like the leading indicators off of that.
[29:12]
Exactly. Yeah.

[36:32]

[36:44]
Um, what we found is typically the reason why, again, most people fail with YouTube ads is because the offer, like I mentioned, right, if you're targeting car dealership owners in Germany, there's only 22,000 of those. It's just not going to work.
[37:00]
We can be the best YouTube ad agency in the world, which I arguably think we're on our way to being if we're not already, but we can't make it work because there's just not enough people that can buy your shit.
[37:14]
So, if your offer is not scalable, it's just not going to really be able to YouTube ads is the best platform. The second reason is creative, okay? Which is what I'm going to talk about next.
[37:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Perfect YouTube Ad Script"
NÚMEROS: 3 minutos ou menos, 5-10 segundos hook, 30 segundos hook máximo
VISUAL: Framework visual dividido em três partes - Hook (30s), Story (2min), Close (30s), estrutura temporal destacada, ícones representando cada seção
[37:30]
So, I have plenty of trainings on this on YouTube, but the perfect YouTube ad script is three minutes or less. All right? And the first most important piece is your hook. Okay?
[37:44]
We all know this. We're all marketers here. David Ogilvy said it. If I was to uh, you know, if I had a dollar and I was going to adjust it, I forget what the quote is. I'm butchering it right now.
[37:58]
Basically, I would spend 80% of my time focusing on the hook, the headline. It's the same thing with YouTube ads. You only have basically I categorize the hook as 30 seconds, but it's really five to 10 seconds at this point now, especially with the attention span.
[38:16]
So, we've had clients that create uh we have every client create five ads each with different hooks, but it's the same story and the close. So, all we're changing is the hook. The first five to 10 to 20 seconds or 30 seconds in this case.
[38:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hook Elements That Work"
NÚMEROS: 5 elementos principais
VISUAL: Lista com ícones - Big Promise/Claim, Question, Call Out Ideal Client, Act Out Problem, Combine Elements, cada item com ícone visual representativo
[38:36]
And the some of the hooks I'll give you examples of real case studies here, but the easiest way to do this would be making a big promise or claim, asking a question, calling out your ideal client or customer, acting out a problem in the actual video, and then combining either of those elements.
[38:56]
So, if you can combine all of those elements in the hook, you're going to have a better shot at getting their attention.
[39:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Story Section - Social Proof & Credibility"
NÚMEROS: 1-2 linhas de credibilidade
VISUAL: Exemplo de introdução pessoal com credenciais, logos de clientes, formato de apresentação de autoridade
[39:06]
The story, which is after the hook, is social proof and credibility. Hi, my name is Brian Moncada. I'm the founder of adspend.com. We spent over $50 million on YouTube ads for names like Sam Ovens, Frank Kern, Bedros Keuilian, yada yada yada, right?
[39:24]
Like just one, two lines and then I'm getting right to the teaching again. So, problem solution, this is and if you've tried running YouTube ads, you probably experienced one of these problems.
[39:38]
Your lead cost is way too high or your lead quality was dog shit right? Typically, this happens because um your creative isn't speaking to the right person and I'm just giving them value on the actual ad, right?
[39:54]
And then you do that problem one through three plus solution and then you close. You do two CTAs, no risk guarantee, urgency and scarcity. This is just the typical script you need to use and follow uh for the YouTube ads.
[40:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "7 Winning YouTube Ad Hooks (Dean Graziosi Case Study)"

VISUAL: Lista numerada de 7 hooks vencedores com texto de cada um, destaque visual para estatística de 125+ testes
[40:12]
And I'll show you examples in a second so you can get a real look at what this looks like. Here's seven YouTube ads hooks out of the 125 plus ads we tested for Dean specifically.
[40:24]
Um which is a whole lesson in itself that I want to touch on really quickly. So who knows Dean Graziosi? Who's heard of Dean Graziosi? Okay, great.
[40:36]
So Dean's been selling since like the TV days when there was infomercials, right? And that was how you sold like direct response before Facebook ads, before YouTube ads, before anything.
[40:50]
And he was so good, right, at selling direct to camera on infomercials to get the average mom walking across their television set to stop what they were doing and call in to buy a book, which is hard to do because they're in their living room and they don't have to click a button. They have to pick up their phone and physically call.
[41:12]
He was so good at that, right? And the point of that the reason why I'm mentioning that is because we tested 125 plus different ads for one of his offers, the free plus shipping book offer. And he's the goat at direct response marketing.
[41:30]
So whenever you have a client that tells you why aren't the ads working and you only have five ads that are running and that's all you've tested and they don't want to film more, tell them to go fuck themselves or tell them that you need to film more ads or you film more ads for them.
[41:48]
Because how do you expect to scale on YouTube ads if the guy who's the best at it in my opinion who I learned from tested 125 ads and you've tested five and it didn't work and now you're pissing at yourself. There's just no way. It's not going to work.
[42:06]
You have to get in the habit of testing, testing, testing, and testing. And here's seven hooks that you can model that made us millions of dollars out of the 125 that we tested. These are the seven best ones.
[42:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hook #1"

[42:32]

[44:58]

[45:12]

[45:30]

[45:46]

[46:08]
And I'm going to share those in a second with you. Um but these are just examples of hooks that you can model. But the big takeaway is what you just said. Yes, 100%. He was one of the best and still is one of the best and he tested over 125 ads.
[46:26]
Every single day we were filming YouTube ads. And when you're spending, you know, $5,000, $10,000 a day, you need to be constantly refreshing your ads way more than if you're spending a hundred or $200 or $300 a day. It's just a different level.

[46:52]

[46:54]

[46:56]

[47:14]
And I would say, I need you to film a hook. I need you to film an ad with the hook saying the law of attraction in the first 5 seconds. And then his ad was the law of attraction. Does it really work?
[47:30]
So, you can refine your ads even more with your targeting once you found targeting that does work by incorporating that targeting inside the hook. And then your cost per call drops, your cost per lead drops even more because it's hyper-specific to that actual targeting method.
[47:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ad Research Tools"

[47:52]

[48:08]
Video ad vault um that we use. If we have a client coming in that's selling a dating offer, for example, we'll search for other dating offers and we'll just look at their ads and see what's working and we'll, you know, obviously rewrite the hooks for our clients depending on what their offers are.
[48:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Filming Process & Best Practices"
NÚMEROS: 5 hooks mínimo, 0-30 segundos por hook, 2 minutos body
VISUAL: Checklist visual de processo de filmagem - Hooks First, Story & Close Second, Off-the-Cuff Filming, Equipment Requirements, layout de processo passo a passo
[48:30]
Um, now when it comes to filming, again, this is a big bottleneck for us and our clients because all of it's video, right? So, it's not like Facebook where you can just write copy and then have an image. If the video sucks, the ad's never going to convert.
[48:48]
So, we always recommend our clients to film the hooks first. We write five hooks for them minimum. When we first launch, each hook's different. This is zero to 30 seconds max. They film the hooks back to back to back to back to back.
[49:06]
We always have them do it on a teleprompter. We tell them to film the scripts exactly how we write it to get in the flow. This is very key here. If you're ever directing people who are not used to filming content, for example, you have to tell them exactly what to do. Otherwise, it's going to fuck up your performance from the creative side.
[49:28]
So, we've learned to tell our clients to film the hooks first, back to back to back to back to back, and then film the story and close second in one piece. It's the body, right? The body is the next two minutes. They film the story, right?
[49:46]
And typically, we have them filming in a different setting. So, for example, if someone's walking around filming the hook in their backyard, we then go have them for the story and the close go in their office in their work setting that's relevant to the viewer and what they're selling to make it more professional and show them the legitimacy because that's when they're actually telling people about who they are and what they're doing.
[50:12]
So, if I was going to film an ad for our agency, I could film the hook pretty much wherever, but in the body, in the story, in the close, I would film it in my office with a nice setting, with a good background, with my computer, with my awards, with everything. So, I'm showing them I'm legit. Does that make sense?
[50:32]
And then we have them film the body next. Now, once they're done filming the script exactly how we've written it for them, then we tell them, "Film five more ads off the cuff now that you've got the flow and the framework down."
[50:48]
And this is where the best ads come. It's not the scripts word for word. Brandon Carter doesn't fuck read our scripts. He reads the scripts, but he uses it for the framework. But again, not everybody's Brandon Carter.
[51:04]
So, we have our clients read the scripts and film them no matter what. And then once they're done filming the scripts, then we have them film five more ads off the cuff after they're in the flow. Does that make sense?
[51:18]
This is where the best ads come because it's not scripted. It's not memorizing word for word where they're reading a teleprompter and you can tell. It's I have and now I'm speaking from the heart and now I'm just selling my product to you.
[51:34]
So that's very key if you're ever running ads for clients because that's the best way to direct them to do that.
[51:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Equipment Requirements"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Lista com ícones - Good Lighting (preferably natural), Teleprompter, External Microphone, checklist de equipamento básico disponível na Amazon
[51:44]
And then you want to make sure they have good lighting. You want to make sure they're using a teleprompter. Like we said, you want to make sure they have an external microphone. This is not hard stuff. This is just stuff they can buy on Amazon and have shipped to them in two or three days.
[52:00]
Any questions on filming?
[52:02]
Tell me why do you think natural light?
[52:06]
I mean, like, for example, right, if I was going to film an ad, I wouldn't film it in that corner right there where it's all dark because you can't see me. Yeah. And again, if you have external lighting, that can work, too. But preferably natural lighting.
[52:22]
Just easier to see you.
[52:24]
That make sense?
[52:25]
Yeah.
[52:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "How To Know If Your Ads Suck"
NÚMEROS: 1% CTR mínimo, 2%+ CTR = vencedor
VISUAL: Gráfico de performance com linha vermelha para mínimo (1%) e linha verde para vencedor (2%+), indicadores visuais de bom vs ruim
[52:28]
Uh, and then again, how to know if your ads suck. At least 1% click-through rate is bare minimum. If we don't get at least 1%, we rework the script.
[52:40]
But again, this all goes out the window if the campaign's profitable. If we're 4x 5x return on ad spend, but again, you work your way backwards. If it's not profitable, then you go to why how much the cost per call is.
[52:56]
The cost per call's good but the lead quality or but the cost per lead's good, then you go to the ads and you go to the targeting. You just work your way backwards here. This is always the last thing we check after we've launched.
[53:12]
If you have greater than 2%, you have a real winner.
[53:16] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Tapp Brothers Case Study"
NÚMEROS: 2 anos 5 meses 27 dias de duração, 4M+ views, 5% CTR, ~$500K em spend
VISUAL: Screenshot do ad performance, números destacados em caixas, gráfico de duração do ad, thumbnail do vídeo
[53:18]

[55:48]

[56:08]

[56:36]
So, the point of this being, okay, when you follow the creative process like we just showed you with the script and the filming and obviously you have a good offer and solid messaging, your YouTube ads can last an average of a year.
[56:52]
That's pretty solid compared to TikTok and Facebook. So, if you do it right and you invest in the creative first, which most people try to skip and they go on their iPhone and they're like, you know, not even trying. It's just there's levels and it's just not going to last that long.
[57:12]
So, now before we go to the targeting, anything on the question, anything any questions on the creative?
[57:20]
So, you mentioned you test like 150 creatives before something worked and that's what Dean Graziosi...
[57:26]
No, not before something worked. In the total lifetime of that offer. The total lifetime of that offer throughout that whole offer which was ran for almost three years, 125 ads were ran for that offer.
[57:42]
So when you're like testing new ads and you're trying to like isolate what the issue is, are you thinking like if you see an ad's not performing well, do you think about iterating the hook?
[57:54]
It's always the hook.
[57:56]
We always just change the hook and actually credit to V Shred here because he proves this point to the tee. That company all they do is change the hook. They test you guys probably know like V Shred but um V Shred I remember watching a podcast from the V Shred company and I thought the Dean Graziosi case study was pretty sick.
[58:18]
They've tested I don't know close to 800 video ads for that Jeff dude or whatever the actor is. All they change is the hook and they have a team of video editors that are just changing different elements.
[58:34]
They're changing it to black and white in the first second. They're changing the motion. They're taking subtitles off. They're changing one element in the first five seconds and they're testing tons of ads just with the hook.
[58:48]
And that's literally just the first five seconds.
[58:50]
5-10 seconds, 15 seconds max.
[58:54]
And they've never tried split testing the story.
[58:56]
I'm sure they have, but we haven't found that to be the problem. It's always the hook because the messaging is usually always the same. Hi, my name is this. Here's probably the problem and the solution is this.
[59:10]
The call to action is always the same, but it's the hook that gets people or doesn't get people. And again, I learned this from Dean.
[59:20]
One of his best infomercials he did before he so one of his best infomercial hooks he told me was um so he actually had uh he actually had Larry King, rest in peace Larry King, I think. Do you guys know who Larry King is? Yeah.
[59:38]
One of the best talk show hosts of all time, right? Famous. He flew out Larry King for a whole day. They built Larry King's studio in Dean's office. They had Larry King introduce Dean for the infomercial he was going to do.
[59:56]
And then Larry King goes uh he says some monologue like for a minute hyping up Dean. It flops. Infomercial flops. He invested all this money into that and it flopped.
[1:00:10]
Dean the next day came back into his office, filmed the hook. Had Larry King fly back out after it flopped. They did the whole thing over again. All they did was change the first sentence.
[1:00:26]
Larry King. He told Larry King to say, "Have you ever looked at yourself in the mirror and thought, 'Damn, I wish I thought things would be different by now.'"
[1:00:38]
That hook changed everything. And turned the infomercial back to millions in profit. Just that one sentence. Instead of, "I'm sitting here with New York Times bestselling author Dean Graziosi," which was the original hook, or something like that.
[1:00:56]
He said, he instead had Larry King say, "Have you ever looked at yourself in the mirror and thought, [sighs] I thought things would be different by now?" And that's when the infomercial took off. Just that one sentence change.

Does that make sense?

[1:07:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Targeting Timeline"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados diretamente
VISUAL: Funil invertido mostrando progressão de targeting - base estreita (Remarketing Lists) até topo largo (Demographics Only), cada nível com descrição, setas indicando escala crescente
[1:07:02]
Uh I'll quickly run here because I know we got a limited amount of time. Targeting wise, again, everything's changed. There's no more keywords, right?
[1:07:12]
So, this is what we call the targeting timeline here. Um, we always start with remarketing lists. If you're already running ads on Facebook, it doesn't make sense to go broad first. It makes sense to go after your low-hanging fruit, which is why it's an upside down triangle because the bottom part of the triangle is the very niche. It's so small.
[1:07:34]
There's only a certain amount of people that are on these lists. Okay? But you want to prime your conversion actions for scale. So, we start with remarketing lists first. People that have visited your website, people that have landed on your landing page, people that have engaged with whatever the hell it is that you can remarket them to.
[1:07:56]
Then you have YouTube audiences. These people know, like, and trust you. You know, if you were running ads, I would run ads right to your YouTube channel, Nick. Like, it would be the easiest thing you would do.
[1:08:10]
Uh, and then custom segments, people that are searching for specific keywords. I was searching for LASIK versus PRK. I got hit with the YouTube ad that was about LASIK. An easy example there.
[1:08:24]
And then it goes up and up and up. As you go up the scale, right? If you go to try to target just demographics, meaning you're not having any targeting. It's just people that are 40 plus, your message and your offer has to be so broad to speak to the everyday person, which is why Dean was able to spend $50,000 a day because he was talking to everyday people that want to make money.
[1:08:50]
That's how much you can spend when you're going that broad and how much you can scale without any targeting whatsoever. Does that make sense?
[1:09:00]
So when you start, go low-hanging fruit and then as you begin broadening out your targeting and you spend more money, your messaging and your offer has to change as well. Otherwise, you won't be able to scale past a certain limit.
[1:09:16] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Custom Segments - How To"
NÚMEROS: 10-15 keywords recomendados
VISUAL: Exemplo de custom segment "How To" com lista de keywords relacionadas a fitness online, checkbox destacado "People who've searched for any of these terms on Google", layout de configuração de campanha
[1:09:18]
And so here's a real example, right? So when we start custom segments, these are intent based audiences of people that are actually searching for these terms on Google and YouTube. Just like I was searching for LASIK versus PRK, is LASIK safe?
[1:09:36]
So we always create a how-to segment. This is an easy thing you guys can do for your clients. How to this is very simple. You can use ChatGPT to do this. Google recommends that you put at least 10 to 15 keywords and we found testing also one keyword just works but this is what's working right now.
[1:09:56]
So how to start an online fitness business, how to start your online fitness business, how to get more online personal training clients, how to start an online personal training business.
[1:10:10]
If someone's searching this on YouTube and Google, you think they're going to be pretty uh open to clicking on an ad by Brandon Carter saying, "Hey, stop training people in person. Do online training instead, right?"
[1:10:24]
And this is one of his best audiences that still works to this day.
[1:10:28]
How is this different from what they got rid of?
[1:10:32]
So, good question. So, now this is basically keyword targeting now. So, they got rid of keywords on its own. You just take those same keywords you would target anyways, put them in what's called a custom segment, and you click the button right there where the arrow's pointing that says people who've searched for any of these terms on Google.
[1:10:54]
People actually searching for this. The next time they're on YouTube, your ad's going to pop up in front of the video they're watching.
[1:11:02]
Didn't already exist before.
[1:11:04]
It did. It did.
[1:11:06]
Exactly.
[1:11:07]
Now they're making it more AI based. Give us the keywords you want us to target. We'll go find them.
[1:11:14]
What was the difference before between this and keyword?
[1:11:18]
So basically the way keywords worked, which I loved, was if I had the keyword law of attraction as a targeting option, right? My ad would show up on any video that had law of attraction in the title, any description, in the meta, in the tags.
[1:11:36]
Google searches. This is Google searches and people searching for Google specifically. That's one of the best, easiest ones you can do.

All we did was take the same keywords that's working on intent, put them in interest, we just check this button instead. People that are interested in this and that one works pretty well.

[1:13:00]

[1:13:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Launching - Google's Best Practices"
NÚMEROS: 15x target CPA (para Target CPA bidding), 10x desired CPA (para Max Conversions), $10 lead goal = $100/day minimum, $500/day example = 5 campaigns @ $100 each
VISUAL: Tabela com requisitos de budget por estratégia de bidding, cálculos destacados, layout de best practices oficial do Google
[1:13:10]
So, we went over creative, we went over targeting, we went over the offers. Now, it's time to go into launching. Okay, this is actually straight from Google's mouth. Okay, this is their video conversion campaign best practices.
[1:13:26]
This is very important, okay? Because first, what they say relates to the budget, okay? So, you have to have a big enough budget to be able to spend. What they recommend is you set a daily budget that is at least 15 times your target CPA.
[1:13:46]
There's two types of bidding you can use when you first start with video conversion campaigns. There's target CPA, meaning I'm telling Google, I want to find a lead for $10. My target CPA is $10. Find me leads for $10.
[1:14:04]
We never start with this bidding strategy because there's no data in the account. They're not going to be able to find those people as best as they would, which is why we start with max conversions.
[1:14:18]
Max conversions is more aggressive. It's telling Google, find me as many conversions as possible. I don't care what I pay, but it gets data quickly. Max conversions.
[1:14:30]
Well, with max conversions, if you use that bidding strategy, you have to have at least 10x your desired CPA. So, if I want leads for $10, I need to spend at least $100 in that campaign per day.
[1:14:48]
Yes. Per day. So, typically with all of our clients, they're selling a $5,000 offer, right? At the bare minimum, which means we need to spend at least $500 a day to start, right?
[1:15:04]
Which means at a $500 a day budget, total budget for the day, right? We have five campaigns at $100 each. Does that make sense? Five campaigns $100 each because we're bidding for $10 leads using max conversions according to Google's best practices.
[1:15:26]
If you go less than this, your campaigns won't spend. And if they do start spending, they'll stop spending. And if you're wondering why they're not spending, it's because your budget's too low and you're not spending enough.
[1:15:40]
So, this is also why people say, "I start with one ad in each campaign and I break out." Like, you know how people say like, "I only have one ad in each campaign. That's it. I don't have five ads in each campaign."
[1:15:56]
Some people do this. I don't recommend that because then you have 20 fucking campaigns and you have $5 or $10 per campaign and you're not following their best practices and none of those campaigns spend.
[1:16:10]
So you have to make sure each campaign has enough budget depending on your bidding strategy. 15x for target CPA, 10x for max conversions. And again, these slides will be in the Telegram chat so you guys can obviously have all this.
[1:16:28]
Um that's very important though because people try to spend too little and they can't. Again, YouTube's a scaling platform.
[1:16:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bidding Strategy Recommendations"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados diretamente

[1:16:40]
Okay, bidding they say to so they actually recommend start with target CPA bidding. I don't recommend that. Max conversions first. Once you have conversions coming in and you're getting good cost per calls, good cost per leads, then you switch to target CPA because now you have data. Now Google knows who you want to go fish for. So let me go fish for them.
[1:17:04]
Why don't you optimize for calls?
[1:17:06]
We do leads and calls.
[1:17:08]
We always start with we always create conversion actions for leads and calls.
[1:17:14]

[1:17:32]
Yeah, that's just a campaign for book calls.
[1:17:36]
You know what's funny is we never change the conversion action to optimize just for calls though. We've tried that after we've had leads and calls combined. Leads and calls combined always produces better quality leads.
[1:17:52]
One campaign...
[1:17:54]
Google ads you can have uh what's called include all conversion actions in your campaign goal yes you have like a primary and a secondary option for each of your conversion actions and whatever your account primaries are account default primaries are will be added to...
[1:18:14]

[1:18:22]

[1:18:42]

[1:19:00]
And you report that back to... So you use Hyros.
[1:19:04]
We always use Hyros. Yeah. So, we don't have to worry about that.
[1:19:08]
Okay.
[1:19:09]
Cuz it does it automatically.
[1:19:11]
Sounds good.
[1:19:12]
Uh, I think that's pretty much it. Oh, and then they also recommend at least five ads, too. So, again, people that just do one ad per campaign, they're getting fucked. Like, Google's literally telling you to start with at least five creatives in one campaign.
[1:19:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Optimizing After Launch"
NÚMEROS: 14 dias mínimo, 7-14 dias para auto-bidding atingir eficiência
VISUAL: Timeline mostrando período de não-tocar (14 dias), lista de ações de otimização apenas após esse período, layout de processo temporal
[1:19:32]
Okay. So, after you launched and things aren't going well, let's say you launch and things aren't going well. If your campaign's under-spending, right, and hitting target CPA, it says you should expand the audiences, increase the target CPA bid.
[1:19:50]
This is all basically optimizing stuff. If you're using target CPA, um, and again, I'll give you guys these slides. You can use this, but the main thing I want to talk about was the budget.
[1:20:04]
Here's the other thing though, when it comes to optimizing after you launch a client's campaign or your own YouTube ads, you don't touch anything for at least 14 days. You should not touch anything for at least 14 days.
[1:20:20]
Another reason why that is is because if you're running a high ticket book a call funnel, that's not enough time to make a decision based on the sales closer who got that lead a week later, right?
[1:20:34]
And then maybe has to follow up with them and didn't close them. And then you're making decisions on a day-to-day basis after you launch and you don't know if the lead quality is good or not because the sales team hasn't talked to them yet.
[1:20:50]
You need to at least wait at least 14 days and Google recommends that too. They also say remember that it can take 7 to 14 days for auto bidding to reach efficiency. If CPAs are still high after seven to 14 days, then you should take some action at that point.
[1:21:10]
So, we don't touch anything for at least seven to 14 days after we launch a client campaign. And you got to be really clear about expectations with clients about that.
[1:21:22]
And that's pretty much it. Um, any other questions?
[1:21:26]
Uh, just some stuff around like policy because I'm running a specific offer helping people, you know, find better solution to get out of debt. A lot of the ads are like... That sucks, right? Facebook, Google immediately flag it for like I think they call it personalized advertising.
[1:21:46]
They call it personalized... they call it negative financial status in person. I just want to get out of that cuz 13 out of 14 ads get immediately put to limited exposure or whatever they call it.
[1:22:00]
Yeah, you just got to be more vanilla with your copy. I mean, we're running into that with a client right now who helps men fix their marriages and it's like really I mean it's literally personalized advertising.
[1:22:16]
And um we've tried changing the messaging to be more you know um hey if you're a man and... because like he was being very direct before and his ads just kept getting flagged and then we had to change his messaging.
[1:22:34]
So it's basically just watering down the copy or jumping around the idea a little bit more. So you said yours is debt. Um...
[1:22:44]
So the one that works is if you're making at least $100,000 a year and you're still struggling with your debt, you're doing it wrong.
[1:22:52]
And that's running and working.
[1:22:54]
Only one that works. Every other, you know, that has anything to do with like this is how wealthy people avoid debt or this is how... anything is personalized by...
[1:23:06]
Do you have a Google rep on your account like a support rep?
[1:23:10]
Uh, no. Because I just like you said YouTube scaling platform $100k on Facebook. We just moved into YouTube.
[1:23:18]
You should be able to you should be getting reached out to somebody in like 14 to 30 days because you just started and they'll ask you to book a call. Hop on a call with them. The best thing they're good for is getting you approved. Nothing else.
[1:23:34]
Don't listen to anything else that they say. Just become friends with them and just email them when you have disapproved ads and they should be able to uh do a manual review process to get them back up and running.
[1:23:50]
Cool.
[1:23:52]
[applause]
[1:23:58]
How do you think...
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$50M+ gastos em YouTube Ads em 5 anos
$50,000/dia em gastos de ad (pico com Dean Graziosi)
$25 CPA médio, $40 AOV no caso Dean Graziosi
125+ ads testados para uma única oferta
$600,000 ganhos em parceria com Sam Ovens (33% revenue share)
9 membros de equipe na agência
$6M gastos em 2023 até o momento
2 anos, 5 meses, 27 dias - duração do ad Tapp Brothers
4M+ views no ad Tapp Brothers com 5% CTR
$636,000 em 90 dias (caso Brandon Carter)
$221,000 spend total, $646,000 revenue, 3.2x ROAS, $369,000 profit (Brandon Carter)
1.4M views no ad Brandon Carter
1M+ TAM mínimo recomendado
$500/dia budget mínimo recomendado ($5K offer)
5 campaigns @ $100 cada (estrutura recomendada)
10x desired CPA para Max Conversions bidding
15x target CPA para Target CPA bidding
14 dias mínimo sem tocar em campanhas após launch
BENCHMARKS DE PERFORMANCE:
1% CTR mínimo, 2%+ ideal
20%+ view rate
$3-$5 CPC (US market), $1-$2 (fora US)
15-30% landing page conversion (ideal 25%)
5-15% video conversion rate
60%+ show up rate
20%+ close rate
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Updates 2023:
Keyword targeting REMOVIDO para video conversion campaigns
Similar Audiences REMOVIDO (1º agosto)
Optimized Targeting substitui similar audiences
Video Enhancements - conversão automática horizontal para vertical
ClickFunnels parceria com Hyros
Ofertas Ideais:
Mass market B2C: Health & Fitness, Dating & Relationships, Make Money Online, Biz Op
B2B específico: Agency owners, coaches, consultants, course creators
TAM mínimo: 1M+ pessoas
Clientes ideais: $250K/mês+ já estabelecidos
Estrutura de Funil:
Low ticket pode funcionar ($1-$27) com backend high ticket
High ticket book-a-call funnel (90% dos clientes)
Free+shipping ($7.95) com upsells e sales call backend
Processo Criativo (80% do sucesso):
Script perfeito: 3 minutos ou menos
Hook: 5-30 segundos (mais importante)
Story: Social proof + credibility (1-2 linhas)
Close: 2 CTAs, no-risk guarantee, urgency/scarcity
5 hooks mínimo por lançamento
Mesmo story e close, apenas hooks diferentes
Testar 125+ ads ao longo da vida da oferta
Elementos de Hook Vencedor:
Big promise/claim
Question
Call out ideal client
Act out problem
Combine elements
Processo de Filmagem:
Filmar hooks primeiro (back-to-back, 5 mínimo)
Usar teleprompter para scripts
Filmar story + close em uma peça (2 minutos)
Mudar setting entre hook e body
Após scripts, filmar 5+ ads off-the-cuff (melhores resultados)
Equipamento Necessário:
Good lighting (natural preferível)
Teleprompter
External microphone
Tudo disponível na Amazon
Targeting Timeline (funil invertido):
Remarketing Lists (mais nichado, low-hanging fruit)
YouTube Audiences (know/like/trust)
Custom Segments - Intent (people searching keywords)
Custom Segments - Interests (broader)
Custom Segments - Websites (competitors + client URLs)
Demographics Only (mais amplo, requer messaging universal)
Custom Segments Estratégia:
How-To segment: 10-15 keywords com ChatGPT
Intent: "People who've searched for these terms on Google"
Interests: "People interested in these topics"
Websites: "People who browse websites similar to" (competitors + próprio cliente)
Launch & Bidding:
Começar com Max Conversions (mais agressivo, coleta data)
Depois mudar para Target CPA (quando tem data)
Budget mínimo: 10x desired CPA (Max Conv) ou 15x (Target CPA)
5 ads mínimo por campaign
Não tocar em nada por 14 dias após launch
7-14 dias para auto-bidding atingir eficiência
ESTRUTURA:
Account Setup:
Google Ads Manager Account (MCC)
Link múltiplos Google Ads Accounts
Conectar Google Analytics
Usar Tag Manager para tracking (simplifica códigos)
Criar 2º canal YouTube para ads (manter analytics limpo)
Brandar 2º canal on-brand com case studies
Linkar ambos canais ao ads account
Integrar Hyros para tracking
Google Ads Ecosystem:
YouTube Ads (top of funnel - awareness)
Google Discovery (middle of funnel - remarketing)
Search Ads (branded keywords - fácil setup)
Shopping (ecom)
Agência AdSpend.com:
9 pessoas na equipe
Contratando creative director + video production assistant
Voando para clientes filmar on-set
Suits toda segunda-feira
Clientes mínimo: $250K/mês estabelecidos
Não salvam negócios, apenas escalam
Problema Comum:
80% dos problemas: funnel + sales team
20% dos problemas: ads
Maioria dos clientes: 5 ads rodando por ano
Solução: Testar constantemente novos hooks
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Dean Graziosi: $50K/dia, $25 CPA, $40 AOV, 3 meses no pico
Tapp Brothers: $30 CPA, $45-$55 AOV, 2x ROI, ~$500K lifetime spend
Brandon Carter: $221K spend, $646K revenue, 3.2x ROAS, $369K profit
Sam Ovens partnership: $600K ganhos (33% revenue deal)
LASIK Colombia: $500 (exemplo de ad que custou negócio)
Low ticket pode funcionar mas precisa backend
High ticket: $5K+ offers típicos
Cost structure típico para scale: $500/dia mínimo
DURAÇÃO: ~1h 24min FORMATO: Apresentação ao vivo / Masterclass completa sobre YouTube Ads
